segs.utf8:
Science is like a giant web where every discovery 
builds on past and present knowledge.

Take Isaac Newton - his idea that 
the same force makes apples fall

and planets move was built on the work 
of Copernicus, Galileo, and Kepler.

Centuries later, Albert Einstein reshaped Newton's 
theories, changing how we understand gravity.

That later helped American astrophysicist 
Andrea Ghez discover a supermassive
black hole at the centre of our galaxy.
And between each of these and other milestones are

countless other scientists who strengthened this 
web, paving the way for future discoveries.

Let's explore this idea in this video through 
four major scientific breakthroughs.

First up: 
How do we know the age of

Egyptian mummies, when the Maya civilisation 
fell or when Stonehenge was built?

The answer lies in Carbon-14 dating, 
a method developed by physical chemist
Willard Libby in the mid 1940s.
Carbon-14, an unstable form of carbon,

had been discovered by Martin Kamen 
and Sam Ruben a few years before.

Libby built on this by proving it s 
naturally present in the atmosphere.

When carbon combines with oxygen, 
it forms carbon dioxide or CO2,

which plants absorb through photosynthesis and 
animals and humans take in through food.

All living organisms continually 
renew their Carbon-14 reserves.

But when they die, the Carbon-14 begins to decline 
at a steady pace, like a countdown clock.

Libby realised that he could determine the time 
of death by measuring the Carbon-14 left in

remains up to roughly 50,000 years old. 
He expected that human excrement would have

plenty of it, so ran an experiment with 
gas from sewage samples and it worked!

Then the scientist tried it on ancient 
artefacts and it matched the estimated

ages for them, revolutionising archaeology.  
Libby received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for

his work in 1960, but it didn t end there. 
Carbon-14 dating has revealed details about

ancient ecosystems, improving our understanding 
of how human activity is affecting the planet.

This informed the science in the 
IPCC reports, helping to raise

awareness about climate change. 
IPCC work, together with former US

Vice President Al Gore, was recognised 
with the Nobel Peace Prize in 2007.

Libby's method not only unlocked mysteries 
of the past, but also provided us a key
to understanding the world we live in.
A world full of technology - one that would

be hard to imagine without the most famous 
scientist of the twentieth century.

Did you know we can thank 
Einstein, in part, for things
like smartphone cameras and solar panels?
You probably know him best for his theory of relativity,

but he actually received a Nobel Prize 
in Physics in 1921 for explaining one of light's
weirdest behaviours: the photoelectric effect!
Around 1900, German physicist Max Planck

introduced the idea that radiation 
travels in tiny bundles.

Einstein applied this idea to visible light. 
For the first time, he explained that light acts

both as a wave and as tiny packets 
of energy we now call photons.

The photoelectric effect occurs when light, 
which comes in different frequencies or colours,

shines on a material and frees electrons, allowing 
them to move and create an electric current.

But here's the key: Einstein explained 
that this effect only happens

when photons contain enough energy to 
knock electrons free from a material.

This effect helps to explain how solar 
cells turn sunlight into electricity,

why X-ray sensors create images of our bones 
or how automatic doors know when to open.

Physicists Willard Boyle and George Smith took 
Einstein s discovery further and transformed the

world of photography by inventing CCD sensors, 
which convert light into digital images.

Other image sensors which do 
a similar job, CMOS sensors,

were improved decades later by 
Nasa for their space missions.

Today, you can find them in devices 
such as smartphone cameras.

Einstein's explanation of the photoelectric effect 
arguably marked the dawn of quantum physics.

This theory turned science upside down 
by revealing that, at the tiniest scales,

the universe behaves in ways far stranger 
and more complex than we ever imagined.

Speaking of the tiniest scales, more than 
100 years ago scientists found a way to use

crystals to see what things are made of 
in incredible detail. That's right...

They played a key role in one of the 
biggest scientific breakthroughs of
the 20th century: X-ray crystallography.
It's so important, that multiple Nobel Prizes have

been awarded for advances in this field. 
But what is it?

In 1912, German physicist Max von Laue 
found that when X-rays pass through

crystals, they create unique patterns.
Soon after, the work of British scientist

William Henry Bragg guided his son Lawrence, who 
formulated Bragg s Law, a short equation that,

for the first time, decoded these patterns 
showing where atoms are inside a crystal.

That meant scientists could 
map atomic structures.

Ultimately, researchers would do this to 
create new things, such as making drugs,

unlocking the secrets of DNA 
or improving electronics.

The Braggs received the Nobel Prize in 
Physics in 1915, and X-ray crystallography

has shaped science ever since.
Rosalind Franklin used it in her

famous Photo 51 which helped reveal 
the double-helix structure of DNA.

Dorothy Hodgkin revolutionised medicine 
by revealing the structures of insulin,

vitamin B12 and penicillin enabling 
the production of life-saving drugs
and improved medical treatments.
She was awarded the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1964.

X-ray crystallography 
has even made it to Mars!

Nasa's Curiosity rover used it 
to study minerals and search for
signs of ancient water on the planet.
From deep space to here on Earth, another
discovery connects everything together.
What do popcorn, WI-FI and the Big Bang have in common?
They all involve microwaves!
You can think of these waves as extremely energetic radio waves.
In a microwave oven, water molecules

in food absorb microwaves at a 
frequency of about 2.4 gigahertz.

This makes molecules vibrate and create heat. 
That s why popcorn pops!

Wi-Fi uses the same frequency, but at much 
lower power levels to carry lots of data and
connect our devices without any wires.
Long before the Internet, in the late 1800s,

Indian scientist Jagadish Chandra Bose 
pioneered microwave technology.

One of his devices even made 
waves like those in 5G today.
Yet, Bose's work has been largely forgotten.
In 1930s, Italian engineer Guglielmo Marconi made

a telephone that used microwaves 
the first of its kind to link
the Vatican to the Pope's summer home.
World War II pushed microwave technology forward,
with the invention of the magnetron.

This device enabled advanced radar systems, 
giving the Allies a big advantage.
Soon after, American engineer Percy Spencer noticed
a confectionary bar had melted in his pocket as he passed near a

magnetron in his lab, leading to the 
invention of the microwave oven.
But microwaves exist beyond our planet.
In 1964, Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson found

the cosmic microwave background, an echo of 
the Big Bang that still fills the universe
13.8 billion years later!
That discovery earned them a Nobel Prize in Physics.
How amazing it is that today the very waves that connect our devices also connect us to the origin of the universe.
